title: Delirium

sections.title: Definition & Epidemiology
sections.content: Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention and awareness, with additional cognitive deficits, not better explained by pre-existing dementia. It represents acute brain failure and is a medical emergency.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 10-30% of hospitalized elderly, 60-80% of ICU patients
Associated with 2-3x increased mortality
Prolongs hospital stay by 5-10 days
Up to 40% of delirium is preventable
Often unrecognized (30-70% missed)
Can cause permanent cognitive decline even after resolution

sections.title: Subtypes
sections.content: Delirium presents in three motor subtypes with different recognition patterns and prognoses.

subsections.name: Hypoactive (50%)
items: Often MISSED (mistaken for depression or fatigue)
More common in elderly, metabolic causes

subsections.name: Mixed (25%)
items: May be agitated at night, somnolent during day

sections.title: Diagnosis: CAM & CAM-ICU
sections.content: The Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) is the gold standard screening tool. CAM-ICU is adapted for intubated/ICU patients.

subsections.name: CAM Criteria (Need 1+2 AND either 3 OR 4)
items: 4. Altered LEVEL of consciousness (anything other than alert)

subsections.name: CAM-ICU Adaptations
items: Uses RASS (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale) for consciousness
Tests attention with letter recognition or picture tasks
Can be used in intubated patients
If RASS -4 or -5 (deep sedation/coma): too sedated to assess

sections.title: Etiology: The D-E-L-I-R-I-U-M-S Mnemonic
sections.content: Delirium is always caused by an underlying medical condition. A systematic approach identifies reversible causes.
keyPoints: D - Drugs (anticholinergics, sedatives, opioids, steroids, withdrawal)
E - Electrolytes (Na, Ca, glucose, renal/hepatic failure)
L - Lack of drugs (withdrawal from alcohol, benzos, opioids)
I - Infection (UTI, pneumonia, any source - especially elderly)
R - Reduced sensory input (ICU, hearing aids/glasses removed)
I - Intracranial (stroke, bleed, seizure, meningitis)
U - Urinary/fecal retention (especially elderly)
M - Myocardial/pulmonary (MI, PE, hypoxia, CHF)
S - Surgery/anesthesia (post-operative delirium)

sections.title: Workup
sections.content: Evaluation focuses on identifying and treating underlying causes.

subsections.name: All Patients
items: Review medication list (especially new meds, anticholinergics)
Basic labs: CBC, CMP, glucose, urinalysis
Consider: blood cultures, lactate if infection suspected

subsections.name: Selected Patients
items: CT head if focal neuro signs, fall/trauma, anticoagulation
Lumbar puncture if meningitis suspected (fever + headache + stiff neck)

subsections.name: NOT Routine
items: Head CT in absence of focal signs, trauma, or anticoagulation

sections.title: Prevention: The ABCDEF Bundle
sections.content: Prevention is more effective than treatment. The ABCDEF bundle reduces ICU delirium.
keyPoints: A - Assess, prevent, and manage pain
B - Both SAT (spontaneous awakening trial) and SBT (spontaneous breathing trial)
C - Choice of sedation (avoid benzos, use propofol/dexmedetomidine)
D - Delirium monitoring and management
E - Early mobility
F - Family engagement
Non-pharmacologic: reorientation, sleep hygiene, glasses/hearing aids, mobilization

sections.title: Treatment
sections.content: Treatment focuses on underlying cause + supportive care. Medications are last resort for safety only.

subsections.name: Primary Management
items: TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE (this is the treatment)
Treat pain (but avoid opioids if possible)
Maintain sleep-wake cycle (lights on during day)

subsections.name: Pharmacologic (Only for Safety)
items: Antipsychotics do NOT shorten delirium duration
Use ONLY if patient is danger to self/others, or distress despite non-pharm
Haloperidol 0.5-1mg IV/IM/PO (avoid in Parkinson's, long QT)
Avoid benzodiazepines (worsen delirium) EXCEPT for alcohol/benzo withdrawal
Dexmedetomidine may reduce ICU delirium (no benzos)

sections.title: Delirium vs Dementia vs Depression
sections.content: Distinguishing these overlapping conditions is essential for appropriate management.
keyPoints: ONSET: Delirium = acute (hours-days) | Dementia = gradual (months-years) | Depression = weeks
ATTENTION: Delirium = impaired (key feature) | Dementia = intact until late | Depression = variable
COURSE: Delirium = fluctuating | Dementia = stable within day | Depression = worse in morning
CONSCIOUSNESS: Delirium = altered | Dementia = normal until late | Depression = normal
REVERSIBILITY: Delirium = usually | Dementia = no | Depression = yes with treatment
Delirium can be SUPERIMPOSED on dementia (common!) - dementia is a risk factor

keyTakeaways: Delirium is acute brain failure - always has an underlying cause
CAM criteria: Acute/fluctuating + Inattention + (Disorganized thinking OR Altered consciousness)
Hypoactive delirium (50%) is most commonly MISSED - don't just look for agitation
Work up: meds, infection, metabolic, hypoxia first; head CT only if focal signs/trauma
Prevention > treatment: ABCDEF bundle in ICU
Treat the cause, not the symptom - antipsychotics are last resort for safety only
Avoid benzos (except alcohol/benzo withdrawal) - they worsen delirium

reviewQuestions.question: An 82-year-old woman, 2 days post hip replacement, is noted to be more sleepy during rounds but is up wandering the halls at night. She cannot recall the date or focus during conversation. What subtype of delirium?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Mixed delirium - she fluctuates between hypoactive (sleepy during day) and hyperactive (wandering at night). The fluctuating course and inattention are classic delirium. Post-operative delirium is very common in elderly hip fracture patients.

reviewQuestions.question: Which medication choice for ICU sedation is MOST likely to reduce delirium?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Dexmedetomidine (alpha-2 agonist) is associated with less delirium than benzodiazepines in ICU patients. All benzodiazepines (lorazepam, midazolam, diazepam) are deliriogenic and should be avoided when possible.

reviewQuestions.question: A 75-year-old with new delirium has normal labs, UA, CXR, and vitals. He has no focal neuro signs. Should you order a head CT?
options: No, CT not indicated without focal signs or risk factors
reviewQuestions.explanation: Head CT is NOT routine in delirium workup. Indications: focal neurologic signs, recent fall/trauma, anticoagulation, or strong suspicion for stroke/bleed. Otherwise, it has low yield and may delay identifying treatable causes.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected delirium?

options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in delirium. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of delirium. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing delirium, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old college student presents with 5 days of decreased sleep (2 hours/night with no fatigue), pressured speech, grandiose plans to start three companies, and $15,000 in impulsive purchases. Which diagnosis and initial treatment are most appropriate?

options.text: ADHD with comorbid anxiety — start stimulant medication
options.explanation: While ADHD can present with impulsivity and pressured speech, the episodic nature, decreased need for sleep without fatigue, and grandiosity are characteristic of mania. Stimulants can worsen manic symptoms.

options.text: Substance-induced mood disorder — observe and toxicology screen only
options.explanation: While substance use should always be ruled out, a 5-day sustained presentation with classic manic symptoms should not be attributed to substances without evidence. Treatment should not be delayed pending a negative tox screen.

options.text: Bipolar II disorder, hypomanic episode — start an SSRI
options.explanation: Hypomania by definition does NOT cause marked impairment or require hospitalization. $15,000 in impulsive purchases and severe functional impairment indicate full mania (Bipolar I). SSRIs can trigger or worsen mania and are not first-line.

options.text: Bipolar I disorder, manic episode — start lithium or valproate plus an atypical antipsychotic
options.explanation: This meets DSM-5 criteria for a manic episode: ≥1 week of elevated/expansive mood with ≥3 symptoms (decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, grandiosity, impulsive spending). Acute mania treatment per APA guidelines includes mood stabilizer (lithium or valproate) plus atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, or risperidone) for rapid symptom control.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Bipolar I disorder, manic episode — start lithium or valproate plus an atypical antipsychotic.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Bipolar I disorder, manic episode — start lithium or valproate plus an atypical antipsychotic

coachDx.misconception: While substance use should always be ruled out, a 5-day sustained presentation with classic manic symptoms should not be attributed to substances without evidence. Treatment should not be delayed pend
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Bipolar I disorder, manic episode — start lithium or valproate plus an atypical  from Substance-induced mood disorder — observe and toxicology screen only.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Bipolar I disorder, manic episode — start lithium or valproate plus an atypical antipsychotic.
